learning_outcomes.description: Identify and challenge theory, concepts, and arguments from social scientists about gender and sexuality.
Work boldly with theory from intersectionality, decoloniality, and interpretivism to craft an innovative gender analysis of a contemporary social issue.
Critically engage with ideas about how culture influences gender/sexual relations, ideals and norms.
Apply an intersectional understanding of gender/sexuality to a case study.
Explain and reflect on course concepts that link gender/sexuality to wider social issues and ethical considerations.

assessments.due_date:
3/08/2026 - 19/10/2026

Completed in class time, every week from Week 2 to 12.
assessments.task_description:
Students are asked to write a reflection in class (pen/paper) related to one of the weekly required readings. A total of 11 reflections will be held, 1 in each of these weeks: Weeks 2 through 12. Students must complete 8/11 reflections to receive full marks (Total out of 24 will be weighted to 25%).

Each reflection should be half a page to 1 page and is worth 3 marks. These will be marked on a pass/fail basis.

To pass, the reflection must contain some accuracy in regard to the topic reading and be legible (clear enough to read). No electronic resources or printed copies of the readings may be referred to during the task.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Reflections will be handed in during class time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Choose one of the video options and write an analytical essay of 1500 words (+/-10%) from one of the following theoretical perspectives: Intersectional approach OR Decolonial approach OR Interpretive approach. Define the issue your essay will focus on and identify which approach you are using. Explain, with supporting evidence, the context of this issue. Explain, with evidence, the theoretical lens and a few key concepts you will be using. Apply the theory to the issue, giving examples from people’s statements/narration in the video, and drawing on academic sources to support an analysis of the wider issue. Use UQ library sources only and include the permalink in your reference list. Reference using APA7.

Marking Criteria: argument and structure (Defining the issue) 10; Research (into the context) 10; Use of theory (definition, concepts, examples) 10; style (expression, referencing) 10. See rubric on Blackboard for more detail about criteria and standards.

Video Options TBC

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Final exam will assess your ability to advance an intersectional understanding of gender and to correctly identify and discuss course concepts that link gender/sexuality to social issues and social theory.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to Gender and Sexuality

This session develops some history of gender and sexuality as ideas, drawing on anthropology, sociology, feminist theory, and Critical Indigenous Studies. It introduces some of the key elements of social science approaches to gender/sexuality, including methods, intersectionality, and the structure-agency debate.
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to Gender and Sexuality This session develops some history of gender and sexuality as ideas, drawing on anthropology, sociology, feminist theory, and Critical Indigenous Studies. It introduces some of the key elements of social science approaches to gender/sexuality, including methods, intersectionality, and the structure-agency debate.

learning_activities.topic:
Theoretical Perspectives

This session provides an introduction to some key theoretical framings for considering gender and sexuality that we will be using in the course, especially intersectionality, decoloniality and interpretivism.
learning_activities.topic_description: Theoretical Perspectives This session provides an introduction to some key theoretical framings for considering gender and sexuality that we will be using in the course, especially intersectionality, decoloniality and interpretivism.

learning_activities.topic:
The Naturalisation of Sex and Gender

This week explores the means by which gender and sex(uality) have been made to appear as ‘natural’ categories.
learning_activities.topic_description: The Naturalisation of Sex and Gender This week explores the means by which gender and sex(uality) have been made to appear as ‘natural’ categories.

learning_activities.topic:
Sex/Gender Norms and Divergence

Building upon the previous week’s examination of ‘naturalisation’ we continue to look at norms relating to sex and gender. We then examine divergence from these norms grounded in anthropological case studies.
learning_activities.topic_description: Sex/Gender Norms and Divergence Building upon the previous week’s examination of ‘naturalisation’ we continue to look at norms relating to sex and gender. We then examine divergence from these norms grounded in anthropological case studies.

learning_activities.topic:
Sex/Gender and Indigenous Worlds

This session reveals the colonial and gender constraints on Indigenous peoples, particularly Indigenous women in Australia. We look to women’s roles in resistance and resilience. We consider life experiences and cultural contexts, showing gender at the intersection of historical processes.
learning_activities.topic_description: Sex/Gender and Indigenous Worlds This session reveals the colonial and gender constraints on Indigenous peoples, particularly Indigenous women in Australia. We look to women’s roles in resistance and resilience. We consider life experiences and cultural contexts, showing gender at the intersection of historical processes.

learning_activities.topic:
Law, Culture and Human Rights

Human rights for all? We put universalist concepts of rights and equality to the test this week, exploring feminism and the travel of human rights – How do rights and the law work together, and who gets included and excluded as a result? How do rights ‘make’ gender ? And what happens when rights come into conflict with each other? We examine human rights in different cultural contexts, asking how else people might conceptualise ideas of care and protection outside the law as we have it now?
learning_activities.topic_description: Law, Culture and Human Rights Human rights for all? We put universalist concepts of rights and equality to the test this week, exploring feminism and the travel of human rights – How do rights and the law work together, and who gets included and excluded as a result? How do rights ‘make’ gender ? And what happens when rights come into conflict with each other? We examine human rights in different cultural contexts, asking how else people might conceptualise ideas of care and protection outside the law as we have it now?

learning_activities.topic:
Masculinities

This session explores how masculinities are socially constructed. We examine masculinity’s links with political and social power, and problematise ideas of ‘hegemonic’ masculinity.
learning_activities.topic_description: Masculinities This session explores how masculinities are socially constructed. We examine masculinity’s links with political and social power, and problematise ideas of ‘hegemonic’ masculinity.

learning_activities.topic:
Love, Care and Intimacy

In this session, we will discuss the work of love, care and intimacy. We explore why some forms of intimacy are normatively excluded from capitalist markets, and examine what happens when these norms are breached. Ethnographic studies of sex work will be used to ground these discussions.
learning_activities.topic_description: Love, Care and Intimacy In this session, we will discuss the work of love, care and intimacy. We explore why some forms of intimacy are normatively excluded from capitalist markets, and examine what happens when these norms are breached. Ethnographic studies of sex work will be used to ground these discussions.

learning_activities.topic:
Sex/Gender and Capitalism

This week looks to the ways in which gender and sexual differences are at once taken advantage of by capitalist markets for profit maximisation and at the same time produced through capitalist worksites. While critiquing the coercive nature of capitalist enterprise, we similarly explore the spaces for self-expression that economic liberalisation make possible.
learning_activities.topic_description: Sex/Gender and Capitalism This week looks to the ways in which gender and sexual differences are at once taken advantage of by capitalist markets for profit maximisation and at the same time produced through capitalist worksites. While critiquing the coercive nature of capitalist enterprise, we similarly explore the spaces for self-expression that economic liberalisation make possible.

learning_activities.topic:
The Politics of Sex and Gender

In this session we look to both the micro- and macro-politics of sex and gender. We examine the interpersonal politics of contesting sexual and gender norms, but also unpack how state policies are implicated in, seek to reaffirm, or rely upon specific expressions of gender and sexuality.
learning_activities.topic_description: The Politics of Sex and Gender In this session we look to both the micro- and macro-politics of sex and gender. We examine the interpersonal politics of contesting sexual and gender norms, but also unpack how state policies are implicated in, seek to reaffirm, or rely upon specific expressions of gender and sexuality.

learning_activities.topic:
Sex and Gender in the Kitchen

This session looks at gender, race, coloniality and tradition in the context of a popular everyday activity – cooking!
learning_activities.topic_description: Sex and Gender in the Kitchen This session looks at gender, race, coloniality and tradition in the context of a popular everyday activity – cooking!

learning_activities.topic:
Abolish the Family!

In week 12 we critically examine the emergent movement of family abolition. We unpack its historical antecedents and explore its rationales – thinking through the family as both a site and mechanism of unfreedom.
learning_activities.topic_description: Abolish the Family! In week 12 we critically examine the emergent movement of family abolition. We unpack its historical antecedents and explore its rationales – thinking through the family as both a site and mechanism of unfreedom.

learning_activities.topic:
Course Conclusion

Course recap and exam preparation
